title: Every Second Has a Plane In It
tagline: A crowded sector, a converging pair of aircraft, and eleven seconds to make the right call.

wake_up.text: Third day of a rotating shift schedule that never quite lets your body settle. Today's sector briefing mentions a weather system moving in by afternoon.

choices.text: Take the time for your full pre-shift rest routine
Skip it and get to the tower early instead

shift_briefing.text: Shift briefing: a busy sector today, a runway closure elsewhere in the region pushing extra traffic your way, and that weather system to track.

choices.text: Review the full projected traffic plan in detail
Get straight to position and adapt as it comes

radar_handoff.text: The night controller hands off the sector: six aircraft currently in your airspace, three more inbound in the next ten minutes.

choices.text: Take the handoff

morning_rush.text: Traffic builds fast. Headsets on, eyes on the scope, the rhythm of call signs and headings that becomes automatic after enough years — until it isn't.

choices.text: Space incoming traffic with extra conservative margins
Run tighter spacing to keep the flow efficient

converging_aircraft.text: Two aircraft on converging headings, closer than the plan called for — a pilot read back a heading wrong ninety seconds ago and nobody caught it until now. The scope shows maybe eleven seconds before separation matters.

choices.text: Issue an immediate turn command to the nearer aircraft
Issue an immediate altitude change instead of a turn
Hold both aircraft's course one more second to verify the read before acting
Call your supervisor over immediately to confirm the call

converge_outcome_turn.text: The pilot reads back and turns instantly. Separation holds with room to spare. Your voice never changed pitch the entire time — that's the part that matters most.

choices.text: Log the event and keep working the sector

converge_outcome_altitude.text: The climb clears the conflict cleanly, and both aircraft continue with separation intact. A clean, correct call under real time pressure.

choices.text: Log the event and keep working the sector

converge_outcome_hold.text: The extra second confirms your read was right — and costs precious margin doing it. The turn command that follows still resolves it, but with far less room than it should have had.

choices.text: Log the event and keep working the sector

converge_outcome_supervisor.text: Your supervisor confirms the call a beat later than you needed. Separation holds, barely, and the after-action conversation is going to ask why you didn't act on your own read.

choices.text: Log the event and keep working the sector

weather_reroute.text: The forecast weather system arrives early, cutting through the middle of your sector. Half a dozen aircraft need new routing around it.

choices.text: Start rerouting traffic well ahead of the weather
Wait to see how the weather actually develops before rerouting

medical_emergency.text: A pilot keys up: "Declaring a medical emergency, passenger unresponsive, requesting priority handling." Every other aircraft in your sector needs to move to let them through.

choices.text: Clear immediate priority handling for the emergency aircraft
Work them into the normal sequence with minor priority

runway_incursion.text: A ground vehicle crosses onto an active runway just as an aircraft is cleared for takeoff roll. You have seconds to stop it or let it continue.

choices.text: Immediately abort the takeoff clearance
Trust the timing margin and let the takeoff continue

shift_wind_down.text: Traffic thins as the afternoon push clears out. The last hour of a shift like this always feels strangely quiet by comparison.

choices.text: Ease toward the handoff

handoff_next_shift.text: The evening controller arrives for handoff. Today's sector log has more in it than most.

choices.text: Walk through every notable event in detail
Give the short version and let the log speak for itself

end_shift.text: Headset off. Every aircraft that entered your sector today left it exactly where it was supposed to go.

choices.text: Head home

random_moment.text: The sector settles in after handoff — the first few minutes of any shift always play out a little differently.

choices.text: The night controller left an unusually thorough handoff note
A personal phone call almost gets through before you silence it
A rare quiet minute for coffee before traffic builds

triumphant.title: Nothing Got Past You
triumphant.blurb: Every call was clean, every separation held with room to spare. This is the shift the tower talks about at the next shift-change briefing.

steady_hand.title: Solid Sector
steady_hand.blurb: A busy day worked cleanly. Nobody outside the tower will ever know how much happened up there today — which is exactly the job.

ordinary_day.title: Routine Traffic
ordinary_day.blurb: Busy, uneventful, forgettable in the best way. Tomorrow's sector looks about the same.

burned_out.title: Redlined
burned_out.blurb: You got every aircraft down safely, but you're running on fumes. Fatigue in this job is its own kind of hazard — log it, and rest.

written_up.title: Incident Review
written_up.blurb: A few of today's calls are heading into a formal review. Everyone landed safely. The facility still wants to understand the margins.
